items.question: The most reliable metric for comparing two versions of a query is:
options: Logical reads — pages touched, independent of cache and load
items.explanation: Elapsed time varies with caching and server load; logical reads measures the work the plan actually does.
items.relatedLessonTitle: How SQL Server Stores Data: Pages, Extents, Heaps

items.question: How many clustered indexes can a table have?
options: One — the leaf level IS the table rows in key order
items.explanation: The clustered index physically orders the rows; data can exist in only one physical order.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Clustered Indexes: The Table IS the Index

items.question: SELECT * hurts index design because:
options: No nonclustered index can cover every column — key lookups or scans become inevitable
items.explanation: Covering queries need the selected columns in the index; * makes that impossible. Project only what you need.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Nonclustered Indexes and the Key Lookup Tax

items.question: An Index Seek differs from an Index Scan in that a seek:
options: Navigates the B-tree to specific rows — cost proportional to rows returned
Is always chosen when an index exists
items.explanation: Seek = targeted navigation; scan = read everything. Neither is universally better — selectivity decides.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Seeks vs Scans: Selectivity Decides

items.question: Every foreign key column should usually be indexed because:
options: Joins on it and parent DELETE checks otherwise scan the child table (inside a transaction!)
items.explanation: The FK check needs child rows by parent id; without an index that's a full scan holding locks.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Blocking and Locking: Diagnosis First Aid

items.question: WITH (NOLOCK) can return:
options: Uncommitted data that later rolls back — plus skipped or double-read rows during page splits
items.explanation: It's READ UNCOMMITTED: dirty reads plus scan anomalies. Acceptable for rough monitoring, never for correctness.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Isolation Levels: What Locks Are Held When

items.question: When analyzing a slow query in SSMS you should prefer:
options: The estimated plan — it's faster to get
The actual plan (Ctrl+M) — it includes real row counts to compare against estimates
items.explanation: Estimate-vs-actual mismatch is the single most diagnostic signal in a plan.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Reading Execution Plans: Your Debugger for SQL

items.question: WHERE YEAR(OrderDate) = 2026 scans despite an index on OrderDate. The fix is:
items.explanation: Functions on columns are unSARGable — the half-open date range preserves index order and seeks.
items.relatedLessonTitle: SARGability: Don't Wrap Columns in Functions

items.question: A .NET string parameter queries a VARCHAR column and causes a table scan because:
options: .NET sends NVARCHAR; type precedence converts the COLUMN per row, killing the seek
items.explanation: Implicit conversion on the column side is unSARGable. Fix: HasColumnType in EF or DbType.AnsiString in Dapper/ADO.NET.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Implicit Conversions: The Silent Scan Generator

items.question: A plan shows Index Seek + Key Lookup executed 5,000 times. The standard fix is:
options: Add the selected columns as INCLUDE columns, making the index covering
Force a scan with a hint
items.explanation: INCLUDE puts payload columns in the leaf level — the index answers the whole query, zero lookups.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Nonclustered Indexes and the Key Lookup Tax

items.question: OFFSET 100000 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 gets slower as the offset grows because:
options: All skipped rows are still read and discarded server-side — keyset pagination seeks past them instead
items.explanation: OFFSET is 'read and throw away'. Keyset (WHERE key < @last ORDER BY key) makes every page cost the same.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Paging Done Right: OFFSET, Keyset, and COUNT

items.question: The optimizer chooses seek-vs-scan and join types based on:
items.explanation: Planning never looks at actual data — only statistics. Stale or skew-blind stats produce bad plans.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Statistics: What the Optimizer Actually Knows

items.question: A @table variable joined to a million-row table often produces a terrible plan because:
options: Table variables are stored in memory only
Table variables have no statistics — the optimizer historically estimates 1 row and picks nested loops
items.explanation: Use #temp tables (which have statistics) for anything beyond ~100 rows or any join.
items.relatedLessonTitle: tempdb, Sorts, and Spills: The Hidden Workhorse

items.question: Under READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT (RCSI), a SELECT hitting a row mid-update by another transaction:
options: Returns the last committed version from the version store immediately
items.explanation: Row versioning removes reader/writer blocking — the biggest one-line concurrency win for OLTP (tempdb pays the bill).
items.relatedLessonTitle: RCSI and Snapshot: Readers Stop Blocking Writers

items.question: A stored procedure is fast for months, then suddenly slow for everyone until a restart. The classic cause:
options: Plan cache eviction + recompilation with an atypical sniffed parameter on skewed data
items.explanation: Parameter sniffing: whoever triggers compilation donates their value's plan to everyone. Fixes: RECOMPILE, covering indexes, OPTIMIZE FOR, SQL 2022 PSP.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Parameter Sniffing: One Plan, Many Parameter Values

items.question: Deleting 50M old rows should be done in TOP(4000) batches because:
options: Small transactions allow log truncation, stay under the ~5000-lock escalation threshold, and let other work interleave
items.explanation: One giant DELETE = massive log growth + table lock + hour-long rollback risk. Batching bounds all three.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Transaction Log and Batching Large Modifications

items.question: A query regressed overnight after a plan change. Query Store's fastest mitigation is:
options: Force the previously good plan while you fix the root cause
items.explanation: Plan forcing pins the known-good plan surgically — reversible, no cache-wide side effects.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Query Store: Your Performance Flight Recorder

items.question: A nonclustered columnstore index on a hot OLTP table enables:
options: Real-time analytics: columnar batch-mode scans for aggregates while B-trees keep serving OLTP
items.explanation: HTAP: one table, two storage formats — the optimizer routes point queries to rowstore and big aggregations to columnstore.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Columnstore Indexes: Analytics at 10-100x

items.question: A filtered index WHERE Status='Open' is ignored by WHERE Status=@status because:
options: The cached plan must be valid for ANY @status value — it can't assume 'Open'
items.explanation: Only literals (or OPTION(RECOMPILE)-time values) provably satisfy the filter — a critical gotcha for parameterized EF queries.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Filtered Indexes and Index Strategy Review

items.question: Dominant ASYNC_NETWORK_IO waits usually mean:
options: The client app requests large result sets and consumes them slowly — fix the app's paging/processing, not the server
items.explanation: SQL is waiting for the client to take rows off the wire — almost always oversized results or row-by-row client processing.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Wait Statistics: What Is SQL Server Waiting For?
